sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=34, col=13).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 72, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: VERIFIED(step 2-7): Clicking red block (4x3, ~40px) rotates perimeter blocks clockwise by 1 position. Clicking green block or colored blocks does nothing. Green strip at col 0 shrinks 5px per rotation.
- Object taxonomy: Red block (40px, left edge)=rotation trigger. Light green strip (col 0)=progress bar. Light green block (40px, right edge)=fixed decoration. Colored 2x2/4x4 blocks rotate on perimeter. Yellow 2x2 markers=fixed indicators. Black 2x2 blocks=HUD.
- HUD map: Left green strip (col 0) shrinks 5px per rotation from 64px initial. Top gray area has black blocks. Charcoal background.
- Win pattern: Rotate until green progress bar depletes. Level completes when strip reaches 0.
- Level log: Level 1: Click red block repeatedly (8 rotations) until green strip at col 0 shrinks from 64px to 0. Score=1.
- Cross-level notes: Levels have different layouts but same rotation mechanic via red block trigger.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 64 actions; a strong score needs about 38 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking (34, 13) changed 30 blocks - that's almost all of them! This contradicts my hypothesis that each red block controls a different ring. Let me re-examine.
Looking at the changes:
- Blocks at row 17 changed (top ring)
- Blocks at row 26 changed (middle ring)
- Blocks at row 35 changed (bottom ring)
- Blocks at row 44 changed (bottom-most)
So (34, 13) rotates ALL blocks, not just the bottom ring. But earlier, (25, 13) only changed the middle ring (row 26). Let me re-examine that.
Wait, let me re-read the earlier output more carefully:
After (17, 20) click:
- b at (17, 23), p at (17, 26), Y at (17, 29), p at (17, 32), b at (17, 35)
- S at (20, 23), S at (20, 35)
- p at (23, 23)
- w at (26, 17), b at (26, 20), w at (26, 26), S at (26, 29), p at (26, 32), w at (26, 35), S at (26, 38)
After (25, 13) click:
- b at (17, 23), p at (17, 26), Y at (17, 29), p at (17, 32), b at (17, 35) - SAME
- S at (20, 23), S at (20, 35) - SAME
- p at (23, 23) - SAME
- b at (26, 17), w at (26, 23), S at (26, 26), p at (26, 29), w at (26, 32), S at (26, 35), w at (26, 44) - CHANGED
So (25, 13) only changed row 26 blocks. But (34, 13) changed blocks at rows 17, 20, 23, 26, 29, 32, 35, 38, 41, 44. That's all rows!
Wait, but the earlier analysis showed that (25, 13) only changed row 26. Let me think about this more carefully...
Actually, I think the issue is that I was comparing the state AFTER (17, 20) to the state AFTER (17, 20) + (25, 13). The top row (row 17) didn't change because (25, 13) doesn't affect it. But when I clicked (34, 13), it affected ALL rows.
This suggests:
- (17, 20) rotates some set of blocks (possibly top ring)
- (25, 13) rotates a different set (middle ring)
- (34, 13) rotates ALL blocks
But that doesn't quite make sense either. Let me look at the actual changes from (34, 13) more carefully.
Actually, wait. The output says 30 blocks changed. Let me count the total small blocks: there were 34 small blocks before. If 30 changed, 4 didn't. Which ones didn't change?
Looking at the "changed" list, I see blocks at rows 17, 20, 23, 26, 29, 32, 35, 38, 44 all changed. The ones that didn't change might be the yellow markers or fixed blocks.
Hmm, but this is getting complicated. Let me step back and think about the bigger picture.
The key observation is that:
1. (17, 20) and (25, 13) seem to rotate different subsets of blocks
2. (34, 13) rotates almost all blocks
3. The strip shrinks by 1px per any rotation
The completion condition might be:
- Rotate until all blocks are in the correct positions
- The strip is just a progress indicator that doesn't need to reach 0
Given the "about 38" target, and the fact that I've already used 64 actions (mostly wasted), I need to figure out the correct sequence quickly.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the level has a SPECIFIC target configuration, and I need to find the right number of rotations for each trigger to reach it.
But wait - I just realized something. The "game over" happened after 61 actions, which is the cycle period. This suggests that the level has a TIME LIMIT or ACTION LIMIT, not just a pattern-matching goal. If I can't complete the level within the action limit, the game ends.
Given that the target is "about 38 actions," and the cycle is 61, maybe the correct answer is to rotate a specific number of times (like 23 or 38) to reach the target configuration before the action limit kicks in.
But I already tried 38 rotations with (25, 13) and it didn't work. However, I was clicking the same button 38 times, which might not be the right approach if there are multiple independent rings.
Let me try a different approach: since I now know there are 3 triggers, maybe I need to click each one a specific number of times. For example:
- Click (17, 20) X times
- Click (25, 13) Y times
- Click (34, 13) Z times
Where X + Y + Z = some small number (like 10-20).
But I don't know what the target configuration is. Let me look at the current board state and see if there's any obvious pattern.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. The current state is after:
1. Initial state (after game over reset)
2. Click (17, 20) - strip 64->63
3. Click (25, 13) - strip 63->62
4. Click (34, 13) - strip 62->61
So the strip is at 61px (rows 3-63). The level hasn't completed yet.
Let me think about what the target configuration might look like. In Level 1, the target was "strip reaches 0." In Level 2, the strip shrinks much slower (1px vs 5px), so it would take 64 rotations to reach 0, but the cycle is only 61. This suggests the target is NOT strip depletion.
Maybe the target is about the blocks forming a symmetric pattern, or matching a specific color arrangement. Let me look at the current image to see if there's any obvious pattern.
Looking at the current image, I see:
- Top row: red, blue, purple, yellow, purple, blue, green
- Middle rows: various colored blocks
- Bottom rows: similar pattern
The blocks seem to be arranged in a somewhat symmetric pattern. Maybe the target is to make the pattern fully symmetric?
Actually, I just realized something important. Let me re-examine the earlier evidence. When I clicked (25, 13) after (17, 20), only the middle row changed. But when I clicked (34, 13), ALL rows changed. This suggests that:
- (17, 20) rotates the top ring
- (25, 13) rotates the middle ring
- (34, 13) rotates ALL rings (or the bottom ring plus others)
Wait, but (34, 13) changed 30 out of 34 blocks. If it only rotated the bottom ring, it shouldn't have changed blocks in the top and middle rings. So (34, 13) must rotate all blocks.
Let me reconsider: maybe the three red blocks have different effects:
- (17, 20): rotates top ring only
- (25, 13): rotates middle ring only
- (34, 13): rotates all rings (or bottom ring + cascades to others)
But this is getting too speculative. Let me try a different approach.
Since I've already used 64 actions and the level hasn't completed, I need to be very careful. Let me try clicking (34, 13) a few more times and see if the level completes at some point. If the target is a specific configuration, it might be reached after a small number of rotations.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The level has 3 triggers, and the "about 38" target suggests the solution involves around 38 actions. If each trigger rotates a different ring, and each ring needs to be rotated a certain number of times, the total might be around 38.
But I don't know the target configuration, so I can't calculate the exact number of rotations needed. Let me try a different approach: maybe the level completes when the strip reaches a certain value, not 0. For example, if the strip needs to reach 26 (64 - 38 = 26), then 38 rotations would complete the level.
Let me try rotating 38 times with (34, 13) and see if the level completes. But wait, I've already used 64 actions, and the target is 38. If I rotate 38 more times, that's 102 total actions, which is way over the target.
Hmm, but the "about 38" target might be for the ENTIRE level, not just the remaining actions. Since I've already used 64 actions (mostly wasted), I can't go back. The best I can do now is to complete the level with as few additional actions as possible.
Let me try rotating a few more times and see if the level completes. If not, I'll need to reconsider the approach.
Actually, I just realized something. The game over happened after 61 actions, which reset the level. So the current state is effectively the initial state (plus 3 rotations). The "about 38" target is for the entire level, starting from the initial state.
Given that I've already wasted 64 actions, I need to complete the level as quickly as possible. Let me try rotating with (34, 13) and see if the level completes at some point.
But wait, I should first understand what the target configuration is. Let me look at the current image more carefully.
Looking at the current image, I see the blocks are arranged in a cross-like pattern with yellow markers at the center. The colored blocks seem to be rotating around these markers. Maybe the target is to align the blocks in a specific way relative to the yellow markers.
Let me try a different approach: instead of blindly rotating, let me look for a pattern in the block positions that might indicate the target configuration.
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just try rotating with (34, 13) a few more times and see if the level completes. If the target is a specific configuration, it might be reached after a small number of rotations.
Let me try 10 rotations with (34, 13) and check after each one if the level completes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# (34,13) rotated ALL blocks (30 changed). (25,13) only rotated middle ring.
# Need to understand the independent ring structure better.
# Let me reset mentally: current state = initial + (17,20) + (25,13) + (34,13)
# Strip at 61px. Let me try rotating with (34,13) repeatedly to see if level completes.
# But first, let me check: does the level have a visible target pattern?

# Look at yellow blocks - they might be target markers
seg = current_frame.segmentation
yellow_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'Y']
print(f"Yellow nodes: {len(yellow_nodes)}")
for n in yellow_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//4, sum(p[1] for p in b)//4)
    print(f"  Y at {center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Also check green blocks (not strip)
green_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] < 10]
print(f"Small green nodes: {len(green_nodes)}")
for n in green_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//4, sum(p[1] for p in b)//4)
    print(f"  N at {center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Let me try rotating with (34,13) 5 times and check for completion
for i in range(5):
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 13}])
    if last_action_result.get('level_completed') or last_action_result.get('done'):
        print(f"Level completed after {i+1} rotations!")
        break
    seg_check = current_frame.segmentation
    green_strip = [n for n in seg_check['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 10]
    if green_strip:
        b = green_strip[0]['boundary']
        strip_len = max(p[0] for p in b) - min(p[0] for p in b) + 1
        print(f"After rot {i+1}: strip={strip_len}px, done={last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": "VERIFIED: (17,20) rotates top ring, (25,13) rotates middle ring, (34,13) rotates all blocks. Strip shrinks 1px/rot.", "object_taxonomy": "Red blocks=triggers (different scopes), Yellow=markers, Green strip=progress", "hud_map": "Left green strip shrinks 1px/rotation", "win_pattern": "Unknown - testing if specific config triggers completion", "level_log": "Level 1: 8 rotations. Level 2: 3 triggers with different scopes, testing completion.", "cross_level_notes": "Level 2 has 3 triggers: top, middle, and all-rings"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "3 triggers: (17,20)=top ring, (25,13)=middle ring, (34,13)=all rings. Strip at 61px.", "goal_model": "Find target configuration or strip threshold", "recent_findings": ["(34,13) rotates all blocks, (25,13) only middle ring"], "open_questions": ["What triggers level completion?", "Is there a target pattern?"], "current_plan": "Rotate with (34,13) and monitor for completion", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
